text:
An analytical inquiry into the architecture and spatiality of Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa and the historicity of the 'alleged temple' narrative on the site of the Noble Sanctuary (Al-Haram Al-Qudsi or the Haram esh-Sharif) has been attempted in this paper. Part I provides an examination of the axis and geometric aspects of the Holy Sanctuary on the basis of spatial data derived from the satellite imagery and analyzed with computer. It has been established by the author from remote sensing data, by computer-assisted geometric calculations and evaluations, that the main axis of Masjid al-Aqsa points towards Makkah. It has also been discovered, in the course of the author's comparisons of the geometric structures of Masjid al-Aqsa and Ka'bah that a rare trapezoid plan is presented in both by means of spatial composition as well as that they adhere to the "golden ratio" thereby proving that a material and more than material link between the two mosques existed prior to the Islamic conquest of Jerusalem.

Part two addresses engineering and structural aspects of the temple description in the Old Testament. The analysis points to a number of textual contradictions, some of which are noted for the first time, in relation to the number of workers, the dimensions of the porch, the height of the bronze pillars, and the volume of the sacrificial sea. Furthermore, the specifications in the text relating to the use of square unhewn stones, in particular, contradict the archaeological evidence for the use of rectangular dressed stones at the base of the Western, or Al-Buraq, Wall. The discrepancies uncovered in this respect not only undermine the claim for the existence of the temple on a sound mathematical and engineering basis, but, on the basis of historical evidence, strongly corroborate the Islamic account of the rebuilding of the Prophet Solomon of the mosque there, rather than the temple.
